events.title: México-Tenochtitlán Founded on Lake Texcoco
events.detail: According to Mexica tradition and the Crónica Mexicáyotl, the wandering Mexica people following the sign of an eagle on a cactus devouring a serpent founded Tenochtitlán on an island in Lake Texcoco. The city's founding date of 1325 CE is the conventional academic consensus, though some codices cite 1345. Tenochtitlán would grow into one of the largest cities on Earth — estimated 200,000–300,000 inhabitants by 1519.

sources.name: El Colegio de México — Historia General de México
Crónica Mexicáyotl (Tezozomoc, c.1609)

events.title: Triple Alliance Founded — Aztec Empire Expands
events.detail: Itzcoatl of Tenochtitlán, Nezahualcoyotl of Texcoco, and Totoquihuaztli of Tlacopan formed the Triple Alliance (Excan Tlahtoloyan), overthrowing Tepanec domination. The alliance became the Aztec Empire's governing structure, with Tenochtitlán receiving two-fifths of tribute revenues. Under this arrangement the empire would eventually control 80,000–200,000 km² and 5–6 million people.

sources.name: Frances Berdan, The Aztecs of Central Mexico (2005)
INAH — Museo del Templo Mayor

events.title: Moctezuma I Expands the Empire South — Flower Wars Institutionalized
events.detail: Moctezuma Ilhuicamina ('the elder') ascended as the fifth Aztec ruler and launched major military campaigns expanding imperial control south into Oaxaca and the Gulf Coast. He institutionalized the xochiyaoyotl (Flower Wars) — ritual conflicts with Tlaxcala and other rivals designed to capture warriors for sacrifice. His reign established the tribute-based imperial economy that would define the empire until 1521.

sources.name: Nigel Davies, The Aztecs (1973)
Bernal Díaz del Castillo, Historia Verdadera de la Conquista (1568)

events.title: Templo Mayor Consecrated Under Ahuitzotl — Mass Sacrifice
events.detail: Emperor Ahuitzotl consecrated the enlarged Templo Mayor in Tenochtitlán in a ceremony that Mexica records claim involved 20,000 sacrificial victims over four days, though modern scholars consider this figure exaggerated for propaganda purposes. The Templo Mayor was the religious and symbolic center of the empire — a twin-pyramid structure dedicated to Huitzilopochtli (war) and Tlaloc (rain). INAH excavations beginning 1978 have uncovered 200+ offering caches.

sources.name: INAH — Proyecto Templo Mayor (Eduardo Matos Moctezuma)
David Carrasco, City of Sacrifice (1999)

events.title: Moctezuma II Crowned Ninth Aztec Emperor
events.detail: Moctezuma Xocoyotzin ('the younger') ascended the throne as the ninth tlatoani. His reign saw the empire at its maximum territorial extent and cultural sophistication, but also increasing tribute pressure on subject peoples who would prove key Spanish allies. When Cortés arrived in 1519, Moctezuma reportedly interpreted the event through Quetzalcóatl prophecy — a claim disputed by modern historians who see it as post-conquest colonial narrative construction.

sources.name: Inga Clendinnen, Aztecs: An Interpretation (1991)
Florentine Codex (Sahagún, c.1577)

events.title: Tlatelolco Market — Largest in the Americas at Spanish Contact
events.detail: When Spanish explorers began arriving on Mexico's coasts in 1517–1519, the market of Tlatelolco (Tenochtitlán's twin city) was the largest commercial center in the Americas. Bernal Díaz recorded 60,000 traders on market days selling everything from gold and featherwork to food and slaves. Hernán Cortés later wrote to Charles V describing it as larger than any market in Spain. The market was a hub of the Aztec tribute-redistribution economy.

sources.name: Bernal Díaz del Castillo, Historia Verdadera de la Conquista (1568)
Hernán Cortés, Second Letter to Charles V (1520)

events.title: Aztec Sun Stone Dedicated — Cosmic Worldview Encoded in Stone
events.detail: The famous Aztec Sun Stone (Piedra del Sol) — often misnamed 'calendar stone' — was dedicated under Emperor Tizoc circa 1479 and depicts the Mexica cosmos: four previous world-ages, the fifth sun (current era), and the solar deity Tonatiuh. Discovered buried in Mexico City's Zócalo in 1790, it became the most reproduced symbol of Mexican pre-Hispanic identity and is now the centerpiece of the Museo Nacional de Antropología e Historia.

sources.name: INAH — Museo Nacional de Antropología e Historia
Anthony Aveni, Skywatchers of Ancient Mexico (1980)

events.title: Francisco Hernández de Córdoba Reaches Yucatán Coast — First Spanish Contact
events.detail: Spanish explorer Francisco Hernández de Córdoba sailed from Cuba in February 1517 and landed on the Yucatán Peninsula — the first European contact with mainland Mexico. Córdoba's expedition encountered the Maya at Cape Catoche, engaged in trade, then suffered ambushes that killed 57 Spaniards. The reports of gold and populous cities that Córdoba brought back to Cuba directly inspired the Grijalva (1518) and Cortés (1519) expeditions. Córdoba died of his wounds within months of returning.

sources.name: Bernal Díaz del Castillo, Historia Verdadera de la Conquista (1568)
Robert Chamberlain, The Conquest and Colonization of Yucatán (1948)

events.title: Cortés Lands at Veracruz — Ignores Governor's Orders, Burns Ships
events.detail: Hernán Cortés landed on Mexico's Gulf Coast on Good Friday, April 22, 1519, founding the settlement Villa Rica de la Vera Cruz. Disobeying Governor Diego Velázquez of Cuba, Cortés ordered his ships burned (or scuttled) to prevent his men from retreating. He received Malintzin ('La Malinche'), a Nahua woman who became his interpreter and companion — a figure central to the conquest and to enduring debates about indigenous agency versus betrayal.

sources.name: Hugh Thomas, Conquest: Cortés, Montezuma and the Fall of Old Mexico (1993)
Hernán Cortés, Cartas de Relación (1519–1526)

events.title: Tlaxcala Alliance Sealed — Key to Conquest
events.detail: After defeating Tlaxcala in battle, Cortés allied with this city-state that had long resisted Aztec domination. The Tlaxcalans, who hated Aztec tribute demands and ritual warfare, provided between 20,000 and 200,000 warriors — the military backbone of the conquest. Modern Mexican historiography grapples with Tlaxcalan agency: were they collaborators, victims of colonial narrative, or pragmatic actors choosing the lesser of two imperial powers?

sources.name: Frances Karttunen, Between Worlds (1994)
Lienzo de Tlaxcala (c.1550)

events.title: Cholula Massacre — Thousands Killed by Spanish and Tlaxcalan Forces
events.detail: Before entering Tenochtitlán, Cortés ordered a massacre of the nobility and population of Cholula — one of Mesoamerica's most sacred cities and pilgrimage sites. Claiming he discovered a plot against the Spanish, Cortés and his Tlaxcalan allies killed an estimated 3,000–30,000 Cholutecs (sources vary wildly) in the city's main plaza. The massacre eliminated a potentially dangerous ally of Tenochtitlán and demonstrated Spanish military ruthlessness to other city-states.

sources.name: Diego Muñoz Camargo, Historia de Tlaxcala (c.1585)
Bernal Díaz del Castillo, Historia Verdadera (1568)

events.title: Cortés Enters Tenochtitlán — Moctezuma II Welcomes Him
events.detail: Hernán Cortés crossed the causeway into Tenochtitlán on November 8, 1519, greeted by Moctezuma II in a famous meeting at the causeway entrance. Whether Moctezuma truly submitted or was playing a diplomatic game remains debated. Within weeks Cortés had Moctezuma under effective house arrest. Contemporary observers from both sides left conflicting accounts; the 'submission' narrative was largely constructed post-conquest to justify Spanish legitimacy.

sources.name: Hernán Cortés, Second Letter to Charles V (1520)
Florentine Codex, Book 12 (Sahagún, c.1577)

events.title: La Noche Triste — Spanish Expelled from Tenochtitlán
events.detail: After Moctezuma II died (circumstances disputed: Spanish say stoning by his own people; Mexica say murdered by Spanish), the new emperor Cuitláhuac led a massive Aztec uprising. On the night of June 30–July 1, 1520, Cortés attempted to flee Tenochtitlán with his army and gold. Caught on the causeways, the Spanish suffered catastrophic losses — some 800–1,000 Spanish dead and thousands of Tlaxcalan allies killed. Cortés reportedly wept under a tree ('La Noche Triste'). He quickly regrouped and counterattacked.

sources.name: Bernal Díaz del Castillo, Historia Verdadera (1568)
Instituto de Investigaciones Históricas, UNAM

events.title: Smallpox Pandemic Kills Cuitláhuac and Ravages Indigenous Population
events.detail: Variola major (smallpox) spread from Cuba to the Mexican mainland in 1520 with devastating effect on populations with no prior exposure. Emperor Cuitláhuac, who had successfully expelled the Spanish, died of smallpox just 80 days into his reign. His successor Cuauhtémoc led the defense of Tenochtitlán in a population already devastated by disease. The pandemic is now understood as the decisive weapon of the conquest — by 1530 perhaps half the central Mexican population had died from introduced diseases.

sources.name: Alfred Crosby, The Columbian Exchange (1972)
Florentine Codex, Book 12 (Sahagún)

events.title: Fall of Tenochtitlán — Cuauhtémoc Captured, Empire Ends
events.detail: After a 75-day siege, Cortés and his indigenous allies captured Tenochtitlán on August 13, 1521. Emperor Cuauhtémoc was captured attempting to escape by canoe. The once-magnificent city was largely destroyed — streets choked with bodies; canals filled with rubble. Cortés described smells from the dead as unbearable. The fall of Tenochtitlán marks the end of the Aztec Empire and the beginning of 300 years of colonial rule as Nueva España. Cuauhtémoc was later tortured (his feet burned in a failed search for Aztec gold) and executed in 1525.

sources.name: Hernán Cortés, Third Letter to Charles V (1522)
INAH — Museo Nacional de Historia

events.title: Encomienda System Established — Indigenous Forced Labor Codified
events.detail: The encomienda system gave Spanish conquistadors the labor and tribute of entire indigenous communities in exchange for providing Christian instruction. In practice it created near-slavery conditions: forced mine labor, tribute payments, and community disruption caused mass indigenous death. Bartolomé de las Casas, Bishop of Chiapas, mounted a vigorous campaign against the system, contributing to the 1542 New Laws which attempted (partially and ineffectively) to limit encomienda abuses.

sources.name: Bartolomé de las Casas, Brevísima Relación de la Destrucción de las Indias (1542)
AGN (Archivo General de la Nación) — Ramo Encomiendas

events.title: First Viceroy Antonio de Mendoza Arrives — Formal Colonial Government
events.detail: Antonio de Mendoza arrived as the first Viceroy of New Spain in 1535, establishing the formal administrative structure of colonial government that would persist for nearly 300 years. He founded schools, encouraged cattle ranching and silver mining, and suppressed the 1541 Mixtón War. The Viceroy was the King's representative and held nearly absolute authority in the colony. A total of 62 viceroys would govern New Spain from 1535 to 1821.

sources.name: AGN — Colección Virreinato
El Colegio de México, Historia de México (2000)

events.title: Royal and Pontifical University of Mexico Founded — Oldest in North America
events.detail: The Real y Pontificia Universidad de México, founded by Royal Cédula of Charles I on September 21, 1551, was one of the first universities in the Americas and the direct ancestor of today's UNAM (Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México). It initially taught theology, law, medicine, and arts. The 1551 founding date makes it the oldest continuously operating university in North America.

sources.name: UNAM — Historia Institucional
Cédula Real, September 21, 1551 — AGN

events.title: Manila Galleon Trade Begins — Mexico Connects Asia to Europe
events.detail: The Andrés de Urdaneta voyage of 1565 established the return route across the Pacific, launching the Manila Galleon trade that would run until 1815. Acapulco became the western hemisphere's gateway to Asia: Chinese silk, porcelain, and spices were exchanged for New World silver. The annual galleon carried up to 250 tons of goods. Mexico's silver peso became the world's first global currency, circulating from Manila to Seville to Shanghai.

sources.name: William Lytle Schurz, The Manila Galleon (1939)
AGN — Ramo Marina / Philippines Collection

events.title: Jesuit Order Expelled from New Spain by Carlos III
events.detail: King Carlos III of Spain ordered the expulsion of the Jesuit order from all Spanish territories on June 25, 1767. In New Spain, 678 Jesuits were forcibly removed — many of them criollos born in Mexico who had built schools, missions, and economic enterprises. The expulsion of a highly educated criollo institution, combined with Bourbon centralization reforms that stripped criollos of administrative posts, fueled resentment that would culminate in the independence movement.

sources.name: AGN — Ramo Expulsión de Jesuitas
David Brading, Church and State in Bourbon Mexico (1994)

events.title: Bourbon Intendancy Reform — Criollo Exclusion Fuels Independence Sentiment
events.detail: The Real Ordenanza de Intendentes (1786) restructured New Spain's administration into 12 intendancies, replacing local criollo officials with peninsular-born Spaniards (gachupines). Combined with new trade restrictions and tax increases, the reforms systematically excluded American-born elites from governance. This criollo exclusion provided the social base for the independence movement — the 1810 uprising would be led largely by criollo priests, officers, and professionals denied colonial advancement.

sources.name: José Miranda, Las ideas y actitudes criollas en México (1952)
Diario Oficial del Virreinato — AGN

events.title: Zacatecas Silver Mines Discovered — New Spain Becomes World's Silver Capital
events.detail: Spanish explorers discovered the massive silver lodes at Zacatecas in September 1546. Within decades, Zacatecas, Guanajuato, and San Luis Potosí were producing two-thirds of the world's silver. Between 1500 and 1800, New Spain produced an estimated 110,000 metric tons of silver — roughly half of global production. This flood of American silver caused the 'Price Revolution' in Europe, financed the Spanish Empire, and fueled China's Ming-Qing trade economy. The mines were worked by indigenous forced labor under the mita system.

sources.name: Peter Bakewell, Silver Mining and Society in Colonial Mexico: Zacatecas 1546–1700 (1971)
INEGI — Estadísticas Históricas de México: Minería

events.title: Sor Juana Inés de la Cruz — 'First Feminist of the Americas' Enters Convent
events.detail: Juana Inés de Asbaje y Ramírez de Santillana entered the Convent of San Jerónimo in 1669 under the name Sor Juana Inés de la Cruz. In colonial New Spain, the convent was the only institution offering women intellectual freedom. She produced remarkable poetry, philosophy, and dramatic works, and famously defended women's right to education in her 'Respuesta a Sor Filotea' (1691). She is now on the 200-peso bill and recognized as one of Latin America's greatest colonial writers. Her life represents the creativity and constraints of colonial Mexican society.

sources.name: Octavio Paz, Sor Juana Inés de la Cruz o Las Trampas de la Fe (1982)
Biblioteca Nacional de México — Sor Juana Collection

events.title: Grito de Dolores — Hidalgo Launches Independence Uprising
events.detail: At dawn on September 16, 1810, Father Miguel Hidalgo y Costilla rang the bell of his parish church in Dolores, Guanajuato, and delivered his famous cry for insurrection — exact words unrecorded, but legend holds it included 'Death to bad government!' and 'Long live Our Lady of Guadalupe!' Within days Hidalgo commanded an army of 80,000 poorly armed insurgents. September 16 is Mexico's national independence day, commemorated annually by the President's Grito from the Palacio Nacional balcony.

sources.name: Lucas Alamán, Historia de México (1849–1852)
Cámara de Diputados — Historia Parlamentaria

events.title: Battle of Monte de las Cruces — Insurgents Triumph but Turn Back from Mexico City
events.detail: Hidalgo's insurgent army of ~80,000 defeated a royalist force of 2,500 under Torcuato Trujillo at Monte de las Cruces, just 60 km from Mexico City. Despite the victory, Hidalgo made the fateful decision not to advance on the capital — historians debate whether he feared his force's lack of artillery or feared the breakdown of discipline (the Alhóndiga de Granaditas massacre of Guanajuato had alarmed many moderates). The hesitation gave the royalists time to strengthen Mexico City's defenses.

sources.name: Carlos María de Bustamante, Cuadro Histórico (1823)
INAH — Atlas Militar de la Independencia

events.title: Hidalgo Captured and Executed — Morelos Takes Command
events.detail: After early military setbacks, Hidalgo was captured near Chihuahua and executed by firing squad on July 30, 1811. He was first degraded from the priesthood in a theatrical ceremony; his head and those of three other executed leaders (Allende, Jiménez, Aldama) were displayed in cages at the Alhóndiga de Granaditas in Guanajuato for 10 years as a warning. Command passed to José María Morelos, a mestizo priest who proved a more effective military strategist and political thinker.

sources.name: Jaime Olveda, El proceso de Hidalgo (2011)
Diario de México — colonial press, 1811

events.title: Morelos Issues Sentimientos de la Nación — Blueprint for Independent Mexico
events.detail: At the Congress of Chilpancingo (Solemne Acta de la Declaración de Independencia de la América Septentrional), Morelos presented his Sentimientos de la Nación — 23 articles that constituted the intellectual foundation of Mexican independence. Key principles: sovereignty resides in the nation, Catholicism as state religion, abolition of slavery and caste distinctions, property rights. This was one of the earliest independence documents in the Americas to explicitly address social equality.

sources.name: Ernesto de la Torre Villar, La Constitución de Apatzingán (1964)
AGN — Fondo Insurgencia, Ramo Criminal

events.title: Morelos Captured and Executed — Independence Reduced to Guerrilla War
events.detail: Morelos was captured near Tezmalaca, tried by the Inquisition, stripped of priestly orders, and shot on December 22, 1815. His execution effectively ended the organized phase of the independence war. The movement fragmented into guerrilla bands led by figures like Vicente Guerrero and Guadalupe Victoria who continued fighting for six more years. The Spanish Constitution of 1812 (reimposed after 1820 liberal revolt in Spain) created new political dynamics that ultimately enabled independence.

sources.name: Wilbert H. Timmons, Morelos of Mexico (1963)
INAH — Museo Nacional de Antropología e Historia

events.title: Plan de Iguala — Conservative-Liberal Alliance Seals Independence
events.detail: Royalist General Agustín de Iturbide, sensing Spain's weakness after the Riego constitutional revolt, met with independence guerrilla leader Vicente Guerrero and proposed the Plan de Iguala — the 'Three Guarantees': (1) independence, (2) Catholicism as official religion, (3) equality of criollos and gachupines. The plan united conservative criollos (who feared liberal Spain) with independence insurgents. The resulting Army of the Three Guarantees swept royalist resistance.

sources.name: Nettie Lee Benson, Mexico and the Spanish Cortes (1966)
Plan de Iguala, February 24, 1821 — AGN

events.title: Army of the Three Guarantees Enters Mexico City — Independence Proclaimed
events.detail: On September 27, 1821 — exactly 300 years after the fall of Tenochtitlán — the Army of the Three Guarantees entered Mexico City and the Act of Independence of the Mexican Empire was signed. Iturbide was proclaimed its leader. After 11 years of war, the Spanish Viceroy Juan O'Donojú had signed the Treaty of Córdoba (August 24, 1821) recognizing independence. Mexico became the largest independent nation in the Americas after the United States.

sources.name: Acta de Independencia del Imperio Mexicano, September 28, 1821 — AGN
Alamán, Historia de México, Vol. V (1852)

events.title: Treaty of Córdoba Signed — Spain Formally Recognizes Mexican Independence
events.detail: On August 24, 1821, Agustín de Iturbide and the last Spanish Viceroy Juan O'Donojú signed the Treaty of Córdoba in Veracruz, formally recognizing Mexican independence. The treaty acknowledged the Plan de Iguala and established Mexico as a constitutional monarchy with a European prince to be invited as emperor. When no European prince accepted, Iturbide himself was crowned. The Spanish Cortes repudiated the treaty, but it was too late — Mexico's independence was irreversible.

sources.name: Tratado de Córdoba, August 24, 1821 — AGN
Timothy Anna, The Fall of the Royal Government in Mexico City (1978)

events.title: Iturbide Crowned Agustín I — Mexico's Short-Lived Empire
events.detail: Agustín de Iturbide was crowned Emperor of Mexico on July 21, 1822, declaring himself Agustín I. Opposed by both pro-monarchy Spanish conservatives who wanted a European prince and by republican liberals who wanted a US-style federation, his rule quickly became authoritarian. When he dissolved Congress, General Antonio López de Santa Anna proclaimed the Plan de Casa Mata, Iturbide abdicated in March 1823 and went into exile. He returned in 1824 and was immediately arrested and executed — making him Mexico's only ruler executed on return from exile.

sources.name: Timothy Anna, The Mexican Empire of Iturbide (1990)
Diario Oficial del Imperio Mexicano — AGN

events.title: Federal Constitution of 1824 — Republic Established
events.detail: After Iturbide's empire collapsed, a constituent congress drafted Mexico's first republican constitution, enacted October 4, 1824. Modeled partly on the US Constitution, it established a federal republic with 19 states, separation of powers, and elected president and congress. However, it retained the Catholic Church as the established religion and gave the clergy significant political powers. The constitution failed to resolve fundamental tensions between federal and centralist factions that would dominate 19th-century politics.

sources.name: Constitución Federal de los Estados Unidos Mexicanos (1824) — Cámara de Diputados
Michael Costeloe, The Central Republic in Mexico (1993)

events.title: Texas Declares Independence from Mexico — Republic of Texas Created
events.detail: Anglo-American settlers (colonized under the 1821 Austin land grants) declared independence from Mexico on March 2, 1836, after President Santa Anna abolished the 1824 federalist constitution. The Battle of the Alamo (February 23–March 6, 1836) and the massacre of surrendered Mexican prisoners at Goliad were key events. The subsequent Battle of San Jacinto (April 21) saw Santa Anna captured. Mexico never recognized Texan independence, setting up the future US annexation dispute.

sources.name: Stephen Hardin, Texian Iliad (1994)
SRE — Archivo Histórico Diplomático Mexicano

events.title: Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo — Mexico Cedes 55% of Territory to US
events.detail: The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo, signed February 2, 1848, ended the Mexican-American War. Mexico ceded 2.3 million km² — modern California, Nevada, Utah, most of Arizona and New Mexico, and parts of Colorado, Wyoming, Kansas, and Oklahoma — in exchange for $15 million ($525 million in 2024 dollars). The Rio Grande was established as the Texas border. Over 80,000 Mexicans who chose to remain in ceded territory became US citizens; their property rights were systematically violated in subsequent decades.

sources.name: Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo, February 2, 1848 — US National Archives
SRE — Archivo Histórico Diplomático Mexicano

events.title: Constitution of 1857 — Liberal Revolution Transforms Mexico
events.detail: The Constitution of 1857, drafted by the liberal government of Ignacio Comonfort following the Revolution of Ayutla, established civil rights, freedom of the press, and crucially the Ley Lerdo — which required the Church to sell its vast landholdings (estimated at one-third of all arable land). The constitution also abolished fueros (special legal privileges of clergy and military). The conservative reaction sparked the three-year Reform War (1858–1861). It remained Mexico's fundamental law until replaced by the 1917 Constitution.

sources.name: Constitución Federal de los Estados Unidos Mexicanos (1857) — Diario Oficial
Charles Hale, Mexican Liberalism in the Age of Mora (1968)

events.title: Battle of Puebla (Cinco de Mayo) — Mexico Repels French Empire's Best Army
events.detail: General Ignacio Zaragoza, commanding a Mexican force of 4,000 including Zapotec soldiers from Oaxaca, defeated the French expeditionary army of 6,000 elite Zouaves at Puebla on May 5, 1862. It was one of the rare 19th-century defeats of European forces by a non-European army, and provided crucial time for Mexico to prepare. The French eventually captured Puebla and most of Mexico in 1863, but the May 5 victory became a symbol of national resistance. The date is now a larger celebration in the US than in Mexico itself.

sources.name: Gastón García Cantú, La Intervención Francesa en México (1985)
SEDENA — Archivo Histórico Militar

events.title: Maximilian Executed at Querétaro — Republic Restored Under Juárez
events.detail: Austrian Archduke Maximilian von Habsburg, installed as Emperor of Mexico by Napoleon III's French forces, was captured at Querétaro after US-backed Liberal forces (flush with post-Civil War weapons and volunteers) crushed French intervention. He was executed by firing squad on June 19, 1867, alongside Generals Miramón and Mejía. Edouard Manet's famous painting commemorated the execution. Benito Juárez, the Zapotec liberal president who had governed in exile, returned to Mexico City in triumph.

sources.name: Jasper Ridley, Maximilian and Juárez (1993)
Diario Oficial de la Federación — July 1867

events.title: Benito Juárez Dies in Office — End of Reform Era
events.detail: Benito Juárez, Mexico's greatest 19th-century statesman and first indigenous president, died of angina pectoris on July 18, 1872 while still in office. Born a Zapotec Oaxacan who didn't speak Spanish until age 12, Juárez rose through law to become Mexico's defining liberal figure — he withstood the French intervention, executed Maximilian, and gave Mexico its famous aphorism: 'El respeto al derecho ajeno es la paz.' His death opened competition between Lerdo de Tejada and Porfirio Díaz.

sources.name: Charles Berry, The Reform in Oaxaca (1981)
Diario Oficial de la Federación — July 19, 1872

events.title: Santa Anna's 11 Presidencies — Mexico's Serial Dictator and Chaos Symbol
events.detail: Antonio López de Santa Anna served as President of Mexico 11 non-consecutive times between 1833 and 1855 — more than any leader in history. His presidency saw the Pastry War (1838), the Mexican-American War disaster (1846–1848), and the Gadsden Purchase sale (1853, selling La Mesilla for $10M). He sold his country's territory, lost two wars, and yet kept returning to power. He was finally exiled in 1855 by the Plan de Ayutla revolution led by reformers including Benito Juárez.

sources.name: Will Fowler, Santa Anna of Mexico (2007)
AGN — Fondo Presidentes: Santa Anna

events.title: Pastry War — First French Intervention Ends with Santa Anna's Leg
events.detail: France blockaded Veracruz in 1838 and demanded 600,000 pesos compensation for French businesses damaged in Mexico City riots — including a pastry shop allegedly looted by Mexican officers (giving the war its nickname). Unable to pay, Mexico resisted. French forces bombarded the Fortress of San Juan de Ulúa and landed troops. Santa Anna, called back from exile to lead the defense, was struck by a cannon shot that required the amputation of his leg — which he later had buried with full military honors in Mexico City. Mexico paid the indemnity in 1839.

sources.name: Michael Costeloe, Bonds and Bondholders: British Investors and Mexico's Foreign Debt (2003)
SRE — Archivo Histórico Diplomático

events.title: Seven Laws — Santa Anna Abolishes Federalism, Triggers Texas Revolt
events.detail: Santa Anna's 'Seven Laws' (Siete Leyes) of 1835–1836 replaced the federal Constitution of 1824 with a centralist system, abolishing state legislatures and appointing military governors. The move provoked uprisings not just in Texas but also in Zacatecas and Yucatán. The centralist turn under Santa Anna was reversed and reimposed multiple times through the 19th century, reflecting the fundamental liberal-conservative conflict that would define Mexican politics until the Reform War.

sources.name: Michael Costeloe, The Central Republic in Mexico, 1835–1846 (1993)
Cámara de Diputados — Historia Constitucional de México

events.title: Plan de Tuxtepec — Porfirio Díaz Seizes Power
events.detail: General Porfirio Díaz, hero of the Battle of Puebla who had lost the 1871 presidential election to Juárez and the 1876 election to Lerdo de Tejada, launched the Plan de Tuxtepec revolt in 1876. He defeated government forces at the Battle of Tecoac (November 16) and took Mexico City in November 1876. After allowing a puppet president briefly to satisfy the anti-reelection clause, Díaz took power himself in 1880 then returned in 1884 to begin 26 uninterrupted years of rule.

sources.name: Paul Garner, Porfirio Díaz: Profiles in Power (2001)
INAH — Museo Nacional de Historia / Porfiriato collection

events.title: Porfiriato Consolidates — 25,000 km of Railroads, Mining Boom
events.detail: Díaz's strategy of 'pan o palo' (bread or stick) achieved remarkable modernization: railroad length grew from 600 km (1876) to 25,000 km (1910); silver and copper mining boomed; telegraph lines connected every state capital; foreign investment reached $2 billion (primarily US, British, French); GDP grew at 3–4% annually. The costs: communal indigenous lands stripped by the 1883 Ley de Baldíos; a thin hacienda class enriched while rural wages stagnated; all political opposition suppressed.

sources.name: Stephen Haber, Industry and Underdevelopment (1989)
INEGI — Estadísticas Históricas de México, Vol. I

events.title: Yaqui Wars — Indigenous People of Sonora Sold into Slavery
events.detail: The Yaqui people of Sonora fought a series of uprisings against the seizure of their ancestral lands (the Yaqui Valley) by haciendas backed by the Díaz government. Between 1895 and 1908, the government conducted ethnic cleansing: Yaqui prisoners were deported to work as slaves in Yucatán's henequen plantations, where conditions were deadly. An estimated 5,000–15,000 Yaquis were killed or deported. The Yaqui resistance continued for decades and was a key grievance fueling the Revolution.

sources.name: Evelyn Hu-DeHart, Yaqui Resistance and Survival (1984)
AGN — Gobernación, Ramo Yaquis

events.title: Cananea Copper Mine Strike — Workers Demand Equality, Massacred
events.detail: Workers at the US-owned Cananea Consolidated Copper Company in Sonora struck on June 1, 1906 demanding equal pay with American workers, an 8-hour workday, and Mexican foremen. The strike was violently suppressed with help from Arizona Rangers (who crossed the border illegally) and Díaz's rurales, killing approximately 23 workers. The Cananea strike is considered the precursor to the Mexican Revolution — demonstrating that even the most 'modern' Porfirian enterprises rested on racial inequality and state repression.

sources.name: Rodney Anderson, Outcasts in Their Own Land (1976)
El Imparcial (Mexico City) — June 2, 1906

events.title: Río Blanco Textile Massacre — Workers Shot on Orders of Díaz
events.detail: Textile workers in Río Blanco, Veracruz, refused to accept Díaz's arbitration decision reinstating oppressive factory rules. On January 7, 1907, workers looted the company store; the army opened fire killing an estimated 50–200 workers (sources vary). The bodies of strike leaders were publicly hanged. The Río Blanco massacre, together with Cananea, demonstrated that the Porfiriato would use lethal force to protect foreign capital against Mexican workers — radicalizing the labor movement and accelerating revolutionary sentiment.

sources.name: Rodney Anderson, Outcasts in Their Own Land (1976)
INAH — Acervo Fotográfico de la Revolución

events.title: Díaz-Creelman Interview — Dictator Promises Free Elections, Ignites Opposition
events.detail: In a famous interview with US journalist James Creelman published in Pearson's Magazine (February 1908), Porfirio Díaz claimed Mexico was 'ready for democracy' and that he would welcome opposition and not seek reelection in 1910. The interview was intended for a foreign audience — but it was reprinted in Mexico and triggered a wave of political organizing. Francisco I. Madero took Díaz's words seriously, wrote La Sucesión Presidencial en 1910, founded the Anti-Reelection Party, and launched the Revolution.

sources.name: James Creelman, 'President Díaz, Hero of the Americas', Pearson's Magazine (1908)
François-Xavier Guerra, México: Del Antiguo Régimen a la Revolución (1988)

events.title: Díaz Modifies Constitution to Allow Consecutive Reelection — 'Continuismo'
events.detail: Having come to power on the Plan de Tuxtepec slogan of 'No Reelection,' Porfirio Díaz engineered a constitutional amendment in 1888 allowing immediate reelection. This 'continuismo' institutionalized personal rule. Díaz would go on to win seven consecutive presidential 'elections' with no meaningful opposition — each more theatrical than the last. The hypocrisy of the reelection amendment became a central grievance for Madero and the revolutionary generation.

sources.name: Constitución Política de los Estados Unidos Mexicanos, reform 1888 — DOF
Daniel Cosío Villegas, Historia Moderna de México: El Porfiriato (1955–1972)

events.title: Madero's Plan de San Luis Potosí — Revolution Begins
events.detail: Francisco I. Madero, imprisoned by Díaz before the 1910 elections, escaped to Texas and issued his Plan de San Luis Potosí on October 5, 1910, calling for a national uprising on November 20. The plan declared the election null, promised agrarian reform (in vague terms), and launched the revolution under the slogan 'Sufragio Efectivo, No Reelección.' November 20 is Mexico's Revolution Day national holiday.

sources.name: Plan de San Luis Potosí, October 5, 1910 — AGN
Stanley Ross, Francisco I. Madero: Apostle of Mexican Democracy (1955)

events.title: Porfirio Díaz Resigns and Goes into Exile After 35 Years
events.detail: After the Battle of Ciudad Juárez (May 8–10, 1911) — won by Villa and Orozco's forces — the Treaty of Ciudad Juárez obliged Díaz to resign. On May 25, 1911, the 80-year-old dictator boarded the SS Ypiranga at Veracruz bound for Europe. His reported farewell: 'Madero ha soltado un tigre, veremos si puede controlarlo' ('Madero has unleashed a tiger, let's see if he can control it'). Díaz died in Paris in 1915 and is buried in the Montparnasse Cemetery — his remains were never returned to Mexico.

sources.name: Paul Garner, Porfirio Díaz (2001)
El Imparcial — May 26, 1911

events.title: Zapata Issues Plan de Ayala — Demands Land Reform Madero Refused
events.detail: Emiliano Zapata broke with President Madero and issued the Plan de Ayala, drafted with schoolteacher Otilio Montaño, on November 25, 1911. The plan declared Madero a traitor for failing to implement land reform, named Pascual Orozco as revolutionary leader (Zapata himself would lead), and proclaimed: 'The lands, forests, and water usurped by haciendas... will be immediately restored to the communities.' The Plan de Ayala remains the most cited document in Mexican agrarian history.

sources.name: Plan de Ayala, November 25, 1911 — AGN
John Womack Jr., Zapata and the Mexican Revolution (1969)

events.title: La Decena Trágica — Huerta Coup; Madero Assassinated
events.detail: Between February 9–19, 1913, rebel generals Bernardo Reyes and Félix Díaz launched an artillery battle in Mexico City's center (La Ciudadela vs Palacio Nacional) killing hundreds of civilians in the streets — La Decena Trágica (Ten Tragic Days). Ambassador Henry Lane Wilson secretly facilitated the Pact of the Embassy between General Victoriano Huerta and the rebels. On February 18, Huerta arrested Madero; on February 22, Madero and Vice President Pino Suárez were shot 'while trying to escape' — universally recognized as assassination.

sources.name: Michael Meyer, Huerta: A Political Portrait (1972)
US State Department — Ambassador Wilson dispatches, Feb 1913 (NARA)

events.title: Constitution of 1917 — World's First Socialist Constitution
events.detail: The Constitution of 1917, drafted at a Constituent Congress in Querétaro organized by Carranza, incorporated unprecedented social rights: Article 27 (state ownership of subsoil resources, land reform authority), Article 123 (8-hour workday, minimum wage, right to strike, equal pay), Article 3 (free secular public education), Article 130 (strict separation of church and state). These 'social guarantees' made Mexico's constitution the most progressive in the world at the time. February 5 is Mexico's Constitution Day national holiday.

sources.name: Constitución Política de los Estados Unidos Mexicanos (1917) — Diario Oficial
Arnaldo Córdova, La ideología de la Revolución Mexicana (1973)

events.title: Zapata Assassinated at Chinameca — Agrarian Movement Decapitated
events.detail: Emiliano Zapata was lured to the Chinameca hacienda in Morelos on April 10, 1919 with false promises of a defection by Carrancista colonel Jesús Guajardo. An honor guard opened fire as Zapata entered; he was shot and killed. His body was displayed publicly in Cuautla to prove his death. Zapata was 39. His assassination, ordered by President Carranza, effectively ended the Zapatista movement as a military force. Zapata became Mexico's most iconic revolutionary martyr.

sources.name: John Womack Jr., Zapata and the Mexican Revolution (1969)
INAH — Archivo Fotográfico (Zapata body photograph)

events.title: Carranza Assassinated — Obregón Takes Power, Revolution Concludes
events.detail: President Venustiano Carranza, attempting to impose an ally as his successor against popular general Álvaro Obregón, was forced to flee Mexico City when Obregón's forces rose against him. Carranza was assassinated on May 21, 1920, in the hamlet of Tlaxcalantongo, Puebla — shot in his sleep in a hut. Obregón became president in December 1920, and his administration is generally considered the end of the armed phase of the Revolution and beginning of reconstruction.

sources.name: Linda Hall, Álvaro Obregón: Power and Revolution in Mexico (1981)
AGN — Fondo Presidentes: Venustiano Carranza

events.title: Battle of Celaya — Obregón Destroys Villa's División del Norte with Machine Guns
events.detail: At Celaya, Guanajuato, in April 1915, General Álvaro Obregón applied WWI trench warfare tactics — machine guns, barbed wire, and fortified positions — to devastate Pancho Villa's charging cavalry in two battles (April 6–7 and April 13–15). Villa lost an estimated 4,000–6,000 men killed, 3,000+ captured, and nearly all his artillery. Obregón lost his right arm to a grenade but survived. The Battle of Celaya effectively destroyed the División del Norte as a military force and secured Carranza's constitutionalist supremacy.

sources.name: Friedrich Katz, The Life and Times of Pancho Villa (1998)
SEDENA — Atlas Histórico Militar de la Revolución

events.title: Villa Raids Columbus, New Mexico — Only Foreign Attack on US Soil Since 1812
events.detail: On March 9, 1916, Pancho Villa led approximately 500 Villistas across the US border and attacked Columbus, New Mexico, killing 17 Americans (10 soldiers, 7 civilians) and burning part of the town. Villa's motives are debated: retaliation for US arms embargo, frustration with Carranza-US recognition, or robbery of weapons. The attack prompted President Wilson to send General Pershing's 10,000-man Punitive Expedition into Chihuahua. The expedition never caught Villa and withdrew in 1917, increasing anti-American nationalist sentiment.

sources.name: Friedrich Katz, The Life and Times of Pancho Villa (1998)
US Army — Punitive Expedition Records (NARA)

events.title: Mexican Muralist Movement Launched — Diego Rivera, Orozco, Siqueiros
events.detail: Education Minister José Vasconcelos commissioned Diego Rivera, José Clemente Orozco, and David Alfaro Siqueiros to paint murals on public buildings — the Secretaría de Educación Pública, the National Preparatory School — beginning in 1921. The muralists created an epic visual narrative of Mexican history from pre-Hispanic civilization through the Revolution. The movement became one of the most influential art projects of the 20th century and served AMLO's 'Fourth Transformation' as visual propaganda for social transformation.

sources.name: Leonard Folgarait, Mural Painting and Social Revolution in Mexico (1998)
SEP — Colección Diego Rivera

events.title: PNR Founded by Calles — One-Party State Institutionalized
events.detail: Former President Plutarco Elías Calles founded the Partido Nacional Revolucionario (PNR) on March 4, 1929 as a mechanism to institutionalize power after the assassination of President-elect Obregón (1928) and manage a succession of 'the Maximum Chief' (Jefe Máximo). The PNR unified regional military chiefs, labor unions, and agrarian organizations under one party umbrella. Renamed PRM in 1938 and PRI in 1946, this party would hold unbroken power for 71 years.

sources.name: Luis Javier Garrido, El Partido de la Revolución Institucionalizada (1982)
AGN — Fondo Presidentes: Plutarco Elías Calles

events.title: Cristero War (1926–1929) — Catholics vs. Secular State
events.detail: President Calles's Ley Calles (1926) enforced the 1917 Constitution's anti-clerical provisions: priests had to register with the state, religious education was banned, foreign clergy expelled. The Catholic Church suspended all sacraments in July 1926. Cristero (from Cristo Rey — Christ the King) rebels rose in Jalisco, Michoacán, and Colima, eventually fielding 50,000 fighters. US Ambassador Dwight Morrow brokered the 1929 arreglos that ended the war. Between 70,000 and 90,000 people died, including 3,000+ priests.

sources.name: Jean Meyer, The Cristero Rebellion (1976)
Matthew Butler, Popular Piety and Political Identity in Mexico's Cristero Rebellion (2004)

events.title: Lázaro Cárdenas Presidency — Largest Land Reform in Mexican History
events.detail: President Lázaro Cárdenas (1934–1940) distributed 18.4 million hectares of land to 800,000 peasant families — nearly triple what all previous administrations combined had distributed since 1917. He exiled Calles (who had dominated Mexican politics since 1924), gave unions genuine autonomy, expanded rural schools, and built the corporatist structure that would define the PRI for 50 years. His presidency is widely considered the peak of the revolutionary social program.

sources.name: Alan Knight, 'Cardenismo: Juggernaut or Jalopy?', Journal of Latin American Studies (1994)
SRA (Secretaría de la Reforma Agraria) — Estadísticas de Distribución

events.title: Oil Expropriation — Cárdenas Nationalizes Foreign Companies, Creates PEMEX
events.detail: On March 18, 1938 — a date celebrated annually as Día de la Expropiación — President Cárdenas nationalized 17 US and British oil companies that had defied a labor dispute ruling by the Supreme Court. Cárdenas invoked Article 27 of the 1917 Constitution. Mexico's population spontaneously contributed jewelry, livestock, and savings to pay the $130 million compensation (in 1947 dollars). PEMEX (Petróleos Mexicanos) was created as the state oil monopoly. Britain broke diplomatic relations; the US accepted compensation after WWII.

sources.name: PEMEX — Historia Corporativa
Jonathan Brown, Oil and Revolution in Mexico (1993)

events.title: PAN Founded — Conservative Opposition Creates Mexico's Political Duopoly
events.detail: Manuel Gómez Morín, a conservative Catholic intellectual and former Rector of UNAM, founded the Partido Acción Nacional (PAN) on September 16, 1939. PAN was formed partly in response to Cárdenas's land reform and anti-clerical policies. For five decades PAN contested elections but never won significant office — not until the 1983 municipal victories in Chihuahua, and ultimately Vicente Fox's 2000 presidential victory. PAN-PRI competition shaped Mexican democracy.

sources.name: PAN — Historia del Partido
Soledad Loaeza, El Partido Acción Nacional: la larga marcha (1999)

events.title: Obregón Assassinated — End of Revolutionary Generals Era, Maximato Begins
events.detail: President-elect Álvaro Obregón (who had just won a second term after a constitutional amendment allowing reelection) was assassinated by José de León Toral, a Catholic fanatic, on July 17, 1928 at a restaurant in San Ángel, Mexico City. The killing created a political vacuum filled by ex-president Calles (the 'Jefe Máximo') who manipulated three puppet presidents from 1928–1934 — the 'Maximato.' Calles founded the PNR in 1929 to institutionalize his control. The assassination marked the end of the era when military generals wielded personal political power.

sources.name: Linda Hall, Álvaro Obregón: Power and Revolution in Mexico (1981)
AGN — Fondo Presidentes: Plutarco Elías Calles

events.title: IMSS Founded — Social Security System Provides Healthcare to Millions
events.detail: The Instituto Mexicano del Seguro Social (IMSS) was established under President Ávila Camacho on January 19, 1943, providing health insurance, maternity benefits, pensions, and disability coverage to formal sector workers. Today IMSS is the largest institution in Latin America by number of beneficiaries (over 70 million) and employs approximately 400,000 people. It runs 36 hospitals and 1,400+ clinics. The creation of IMSS represented Mexico's incorporation of workers into the welfare state and the PRI's social contract with urban labor.

sources.name: IMSS — Historia Institucional
Diario Oficial de la Federación — January 19, 1943 (Ley del IMSS)

events.title: Cárdenas Exiles Calles — Ends the Maximato, Asserts Presidential Supremacy
events.detail: President Lázaro Cárdenas, initially seen as a Calles puppet, expelled the 'Jefe Máximo' Plutarco Elías Calles from Mexico on April 9, 1936, ending the Maximato — Calles's decade of indirect rule through puppet presidents. Cárdenas had built his own political base through labor unions (CTM, under Vicente Lombardo Toledano) and agrarian organizations. His expulsion of Calles established the principle that the sitting president — not a former military strongman — held supreme political authority. This transition institutionalized the 'Imperial Presidency' model that lasted until 2000.

sources.name: Alan Knight, 'Cardenismo: Juggernaut or Jalopy?', Journal of Latin American Studies (1994)
AGN — Fondo Presidentes: Lázaro Cárdenas

events.title: Mexico Enters WWII — Axis Sinkings of Mexican Tankers Spark Declaration
events.detail: Mexico declared war on the Axis powers on May 28, 1942, after German submarines sank two Mexican oil tankers (Potrero del Llano, May 13; Faja de Oro, May 21) in the Gulf of Mexico. Mexico contributed the 201st Fighter Squadron ('Aguilas Aztecas') which fought in the Philippines. More significantly, Mexico supplied raw materials (oil, copper, zinc) critical to the Allied war effort and formalized the Good Neighbor Policy partnership with the US that would structure bilateral relations for decades.

sources.name: SRE — Archivo Histórico Diplomático Mexicano, WWII
Sergio Aguayo Quezada, México en cifras (2001)

events.title: Bracero Program Launched (1942–1964) — 4.6 Million Guest Worker Contracts
events.detail: The Bracero Program ('bracero' = manual laborer) was a bilateral US-Mexico agreement providing temporary agricultural work contracts to Mexican men. From 1942 to 1964 it issued 4.6 million contracts (many to the same individuals repeatedly). Intended as a WWII labor emergency measure, it became structurally embedded in US Southwest agriculture. Workers were subjected to DDT fumigation at the border, had 10% of wages withheld (most never recovered), and were deported en masse during recessions. The program created durable migration networks that persist today.

sources.name: Ernesto Galarza, Merchants of Labor: The Mexican Bracero Story (1964)
SRE / US State Department — Bracero Program Records

events.title: PRM Renamed PRI — Single-Party Hegemony Consolidated
events.detail: Under President Miguel Alemán, the party was renamed Partido Revolucionario Institucional (PRI) on January 18, 1946 and restructured around three sectors: labor (CTM), agrarian (CNC), and 'popular' (CNOP). Alemán's presidency (1946–1952) marked Mexico's turn toward industrialization and away from agrarian reform — the beginning of the 'Mexican Miracle.' The civilian presidency replaced military dominance; Alemán was the first civilian president since Carranza. PRI would rule Mexico unbroken until 2000.

sources.name: Luis Javier Garrido, El Partido de la Revolución Institucionalizada (1982)
PRI — Archivo Histórico

events.title: Mexican Women Gain Right to Vote in Federal Elections
events.detail: Mexico's Congress amended the Constitution on October 17, 1953 under President Adolfo Ruiz Cortines, granting women full political citizenship and the right to vote in federal elections. Mexican women had previously been able to vote in some municipal elections. The 1953 reform was partly a response to the Peronist example in Argentina and US cold war concerns about women's exclusion as a propaganda vulnerability. Aurora Jiménez de Palacios became the first woman elected to the Chamber of Deputies in 1952.

sources.name: Diario Oficial de la Federación — October 17, 1953
Cámara de Diputados — Historia del Poder Legislativo

events.title: Mexican Miracle — 6–7% Annual GDP Growth for Two Decades
events.detail: From the mid-1950s through the 1960s, Mexico achieved sustained GDP growth of 6–7% annually with low inflation — the 'milagro mexicano' or Mexican Miracle. Import substitution industrialization (ISI) built steel plants, petrochemicals, automobiles, and consumer goods industries. The population grew from 25 million (1950) to 48 million (1970). Mexico City expanded rapidly; rural-to-urban migration transformed the country. Growth was real but narrowly distributed, with rural poverty largely untouched.

sources.name: INEGI — Estadísticas Históricas de México
Victor Urquidi, Mexico's Challenge: The Private Sector and Economic Development (1964)

events.title: Tlatelolco Massacre — Hundreds Killed Days Before Mexico City Olympics
events.detail: Ten days before the October 12, 1968 Olympics opening, President Gustavo Díaz Ordaz ordered security forces to fire on a student demonstration of 10,000 at Plaza de las Tres Culturas, Tlatelolco. Olympic Battalion snipers fired from surrounding buildings; army troops fired from ground level. The government reported 25–30 deaths; independent and foreign accounts estimate 200–400 killed. Hundreds were arrested; testimony describes mass graves. The massacre failed to prevent a global student solidarity movement but crushed Mexico City's student organization. The Olympics proceeded without incident.

sources.name: Elena Poniatowska, La noche de Tlatelolco (1971)
FEMOSPP — Informe Histórico a la Sociedad Mexicana (2006)
Kate Doyle, National Security Archive — Tlatelolco Massacre documents (2003)

events.title: CIMMYT Founded — Mexico as Birthplace of the Green Revolution
events.detail: The International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center (CIMMYT) was formally established in Mexico City in 1966, building on Norman Borlaug's work developing semi-dwarf, high-yield, disease-resistant wheat varieties at CIMMYT's predecessor (Oficina de Estudios Especiales) since 1944. Borlaug's wheat varieties — developed in Mexico — increased global wheat production by 160% between 1950 and 1992, averting famine for hundreds of millions. Borlaug won the 1970 Nobel Peace Prize. Mexico thus became the birthplace of the Green Revolution.

sources.name: CIMMYT — Historical Archives
Norman Borlaug, Nobel Lecture (1970)

events.title: Mexico City Metro Opens — Latin America's Largest Urban Rail Network
events.detail: The Sistema de Transporte Colectivo Metro of Mexico City opened its first line on September 4, 1969 — a French-designed system with rubber-tired trains. Today the Metro has 12 lines, 195 stations, and 226 km of track, carrying 4.6 million passengers daily (pre-COVID). Mexico City Metro is Latin America's largest and the world's second-busiest by daily ridership after Tokyo. The system became politically iconic when AMLO's allies passed free metro card legislation (2023). A 2021 Metro Línea 12 overpass collapse killed 26.

sources.name: STC Metro — Historia e Infraestructura
INEGI — Anuario Estadístico del Distrito Federal 1969

events.title: Corpus Christi Massacre — Paramilitary 'Halcones' Attack Students
events.detail: President Luis Echeverría — who had been Interior Minister during Tlatelolco — authorized paramilitary 'Halcones' (Falcons) to attack a student march on June 10, 1971, Corpus Christi Day. The attack killed an estimated 30–120 students. Film footage was seized and suppressed. Echeverría dismissed the Mexico City regente (governor) but no senior official was charged. The massacre confirmed that the 1968 repressive apparatus had not been dismantled; Echeverría's subsequent 'democratic opening' was widely seen as window dressing.

sources.name: Proceso magazine — '¿Y quién los mandó?' special edition (2002)
National Security Archive — Halconazo documents

events.title: López Portillo Oil Boom — 'We Must Learn to Manage Abundance'
events.detail: President José López Portillo took office in December 1976 just as massive new oil discoveries (Cantarell, Reforma) transformed Mexico into a major oil exporter during the 1973–1980 price surge. Lopez Portillo famously declared he was now facing 'the problem of plenty' and borrowed heavily against future oil revenues. Mexico's external debt grew from $6 billion (1970) to $80 billion (1982). When oil prices collapsed in 1981–1982, Mexico faced insolvency — triggering the 1982 debt crisis.

sources.name: Lorenzo Meyer, La segunda muerte de la Revolución Mexicana (1992)
PEMEX — Informe Anual 1980

events.title: Mexico Defaults on Debt — 1982 Crisis Triggers Global 'Developing World Debt Crisis'
events.detail: On August 12, 1982, Finance Minister Jesús Silva Herzog called the IMF and US Treasury: Mexico would miss a $200 million debt payment on August 20. Mexico's $80 billion debt was the largest in the developing world. The crisis triggered a peso devaluation, capital flight, bank nationalization (September 1, 1982 — López Portillo nationalized all Mexican banks in a dramatic live address), and ushered in a decade of IMF austerity under De la Madrid. The 1982 crisis is the starting gun of the 'Lost Decade' across Latin America.

sources.name: Nora Hamilton, The Limits of State Autonomy (1982)
IMF — Mexico Letter of Intent, November 1982

events.title: Mexico City Earthquake (8.1 M) — Government Paralysis Births Civil Society
events.detail: A magnitude 8.1 earthquake struck at 7:19 AM on September 19, 1985, killing 8,000–40,000 people (disputed) and destroying 30,000+ dwellings in Mexico City. President De la Madrid was conspicuously absent for the first 72 hours; he initially refused international aid. Citizens organized spontaneous rescue brigades and neighborhood committees — widely credited as the birth of Mexico's modern civil society and democratic movement. The COSVI (Civil Society Council) and multiple NGOs that formed after the earthquake became the backbone of the 1988 and 2000 opposition campaigns.

sources.name: CENAPRED — Sismos de septiembre de 1985 (2005)
Elena Poniatowska, Nada, nadie: las voces del temblor (1988)

events.title: 'Se Cayó el Sistema' — 1988 Election: PRI vs. Cárdenas Coalition
events.detail: The July 6, 1988 presidential election pitted PRI candidate Carlos Salinas de Gortari against Cuauhtémoc Cárdenas Solórzano, leading a broad center-left coalition (FDN). On election night, the government's computer system (PREP predecessor) 'crashed' when early unofficial counts showed Cárdenas winning. When results were announced, Salinas had won with 50.7%. International observers found the election marred by fraud; ballot boxes were subsequently incinerated by a PRI-controlled Congress. The 1988 crisis made electoral reform Mexico's central political demand of the 1990s.

sources.name: Proceso — 'El fraude del siglo' (1988)
IACHR — Informe Anual 1989, Mexico section

events.title: Mexico Hosts Second FIFA World Cup — Maradona's Hand of God
events.detail: Mexico successfully hosted the 1986 FIFA World Cup — stepping in after Colombia withdrew in 1983 — despite the devastating 1985 earthquake less than a year before. The tournament is remembered for Diego Maradona's Argentina, including the infamous 'Hand of God' goal against England in the quarterfinals and the 'Goal of the Century' in the same match. Mexico became the first nation to host the World Cup twice. The 2026 World Cup returns to Mexico (Guadalajara, Mexico City, Monterrey venues) 40 years later.

sources.name: FIFA — 1986 FIFA World Cup Mexico Official Report
FEMEXFUT — Archivo Histórico

events.title: Mexico's Dirty War — State Disappears 1,200+ Political Dissidents (1968–1982)
events.detail: The Mexican government conducted a systematic campaign of forced disappearances, torture, and extrajudicial killings against leftist guerrillas, student activists, and alleged subversives from 1968 to approximately 1982. The Brigada Blanca (White Brigade) — a joint federal-police paramilitary unit — killed or disappeared an estimated 600–1,200 people in this period. Unlike South American 'dirty wars,' Mexico's was largely hidden from public view. The FEMOSPP special prosecutor's 2006 report found sufficient evidence to charge former presidents Díaz Ordaz and Echeverría with genocide — but both died before trial.

sources.name: FEMOSPP — Informe Histórico a la Sociedad Mexicana (2006)
Kate Doyle & Carlos Osorio, National Security Archive (2006)

events.title: NAFTA Negotiations Begin — Mexico Proposes Free Trade with US and Canada
events.detail: President Carlos Salinas de Gortari proposed the North American Free Trade Agreement to Presidents Bush and Canadian PM Mulroney in 1990–1991 — a radical departure from Mexico's century-long economic nationalism. NAFTA was negotiated throughout 1991–1992 and signed December 17, 1992 (effective January 1, 1994). Salinas framed it as Mexico's entry into the 'First World.' Critics (UNAM economists, rural unions) predicted it would destroy Mexican agriculture and accelerate migration — predictions that proved accurate for the subsistence farming sector.

sources.name: SRE — NAFTA Negotiating Records
Maxwell Cameron & Brian Tomlin, The Making of NAFTA (2000)

events.title: NAFTA Takes Effect — EZLN Zapatista Uprising Begins Same Day
events.detail: On January 1, 1994 — the day NAFTA entered into force — the Ejército Zapatista de Liberación Nacional (EZLN) seized San Cristóbal de las Casas and four other Chiapas towns, declaring war on the Mexican government and NAFTA. Subcomandante Marcos's communiqués ('¡Ya Basta!') went global via the internet — one of the first 'internet revolutions.' The Zapatistas, representing primarily Tzotzil, Tzeltal, and Tojolabal Maya communities, framed NAFTA as a death sentence for indigenous peoples. A 12-day war followed; peace talks since 1994 have produced partial agreements but not final settlement.

sources.name: EZLN — 'Declaration of the Lacandón Jungle', January 1, 1994
CNDH — Informe sobre el conflicto en Chiapas (1994)

events.title: PRI Candidate Luis Donaldo Colosio Assassinated in Tijuana
events.detail: Luis Donaldo Colosio, the PRI's presidential candidate for 1994 and widely seen as a reformer who might democratize the party, was shot twice at close range during a campaign rally in Tijuana's Lomas Taurinas neighborhood on March 23, 1994. Mario Aburto Martínez was arrested at the scene and later convicted as the lone gunman. Multiple independent forensic analyses suggested a second shooter; no conspiracy was ever officially proven. Colosio's death — on top of the EZLN uprising — shocked Mexico and defined 1994 as the country's most turbulent year since the Revolution.

sources.name: PGR — Averiguación previa, 1994–1999
Reforma — Investigación especial Colosio (1999)

events.title: Peso Crisis — 'Error de Diciembre' Triggers Latin American Contagion
events.detail: Incoming Finance Minister Jaime Serra Puche announced a peso devaluation of 15% on December 20, 1994 (three weeks after Zedillo's inauguration). Investors panicked — the peso had been artificially supported during the election year — and the peso collapsed 50% within weeks. GDP fell 6.2% in 1995; Mexico's banks nearly failed. President Clinton pushed a $50 billion emergency credit package (without Congressional approval) to prevent the 'Tequila Effect' from collapsing other emerging markets. Mexico repaid the US loans by January 1997 — three years early.

sources.name: SHCP — Informe sobre la crisis cambiaria de 1994–95
IMF — Mexico crisis post-mortem (1995)

events.title: IFE Constitutional Reform — Autonomous Electoral Institute Ends PRI Control
events.detail: The 1996 constitutional reform created a genuinely autonomous Federal Electoral Institute (IFE), with independent councillors elected by Congress rather than controlled by the Interior Ministry. IFE created the first credible voter registry, credentialed all voters with photo ID (an enormous logistical feat), and established transparent vote-counting (PREP system). The 1997 midterm elections — supervised by the new IFE — ended PRI's majority in the Chamber of Deputies for the first time since 1929. The institutional groundwork for the 2000 PRI defeat was built here.

sources.name: IFE — Historia Institucional
Joseph Klesner, 'Electoral Politics and Mexico's Democratic Transition', in Mexico Under Fox (2004)

events.title: Vicente Fox Wins Presidency — 71 Years of PRI Rule Ends
events.detail: PAN candidate Vicente Fox Quesada won Mexico's presidential election on July 2, 2000 with 42.5% of the vote, defeating PRI's Francisco Labastida (36.1%) and PRD's Cuauhtémoc Cárdenas (16.6%). For the first time since 1929, Mexico had a peaceful transfer of power to an opposition president. Zedillo conceded within hours. IFE's credibility made a fair count possible. Fox declared: 'We have achieved one of the most significant achievements in the history of Mexico — true democracy.' The date is considered Mexico's democratic transition moment.

sources.name: IFE — Elecciones Federales 2000 — Resultados definitivos
Jorge Castañeda, La herencia: arqueología de la sucesión presidencial (1999)

events.title: Telmex Privatized to Carlos Slim — Creating Mexico's First Billionaire
events.detail: President Salinas sold the state telephone company Telmex to a consortium led by Carlos Slim Helú for $1.76 billion in December 1990. Slim acquired a near-monopoly over Mexican fixed-line and long-distance telephone service. By leveraging the monopoly, Slim built Grupo Carso into an empire spanning retail, construction, finance, and media. By 2007, Carlos Slim was briefly the world's richest person (net worth $74 billion). His dominance of telecommunications — which OECD estimated cost Mexico $25 billion/year in economic efficiency losses — became a central critique of Salinas-era privatization.

sources.name: OECD — Review of Telecommunication Policy in Mexico (2012)
Julieta Campos, México: El estado del mal (1996)

events.title: FOBAPROA Scandal — Public Assumes $65 Billion in Private Bank Losses
events.detail: The FOBAPROA (Fondo Bancario de Protección al Ahorro) was the bank deposit insurance fund used by the Zedillo government to bail out Mexican banks after the 1994 peso crisis. In 1998, the government converted $65 billion in bank losses (many involving insider loans to politically connected borrowers) into public debt — effectively socializing private losses. The FOBAPROA scandal became one of the largest in Mexican history, representing roughly 18% of GDP. The PRD led opposition in Congress that delayed but ultimately approved the IPAB successor fund.

sources.name: SHCP — IPAB Informe de Gestión (2000)
Rogelio Ramírez de la O, FOBAPROA: El Gran Atentado (1999)

era: PAN Era, NAFTA Tensions & Drug War Origins (2000–2012)

events.title: Fox Inaugurated — PRI Apparatus Resists Democratic Transition
events.detail: Vicente Fox's December 1, 2000 inauguration ended 71 years of continuous PRI rule. Fox symbolically entered Congress through the back door, refusing PRI's ceremonial route. His government opened historical archives on political crimes (Tlatelolco, Dirty War), creating the FEMOSPP special prosecutor. However, Fox failed to prosecute former officials effectively, pass fiscal reform, or resolve the Chiapas conflict. His two-term PAN administration delivered democratic freedoms but struggled to translate political opening into economic inclusion.

sources.name: Los Pinos — Discurso inaugural, December 1, 2000
Sergio Aguayo, De Tlatelolco a Ayotzinapa (2015)

events.title: Disputed Calderón vs. López Obrador Election — AMLO Claims Fraud
events.detail: Felipe Calderón (PAN) won the 2006 presidential election by just 243,934 votes (0.58%) over Andrés Manuel López Obrador (PRD). AMLO refused to accept the result, set up a 'parallel government' in Mexico City's Zócalo for months, and declared himself 'legitimate president.' The TRIFE (Federal Electoral Tribunal) upheld Calderón's victory after a partial recount, finding irregularities but not enough to change the outcome. The episode deepened Mexico's political polarization and set AMLO on his path to a third presidential bid.

sources.name: TRIFE — Resolución sobre la elección presidencial 2006
Denise Dresser, El país de uno (2011)

events.title: Calderón Launches Military Offensive Against Drug Cartels
events.detail: President Felipe Calderón deploys 6,500 army troops to Michoacán — the opening move of Mexico's military-led drug war. The offensive marks a paradigm shift from law enforcement to military intervention, setting off a cycle of cartel fragmentation and escalating violence that would kill over 100,000 people by 2012.

sources.name: Reuters
InSight Crime

events.title: Beltrán Leyva Split from Sinaloa Cartel — Mexico's Criminal Map Fractures
events.detail: The Beltrán Leyva brothers split from the Sinaloa Cartel after accusing El Chapo of betraying them to authorities. The rupture triggers bloody turf wars across multiple states and begins a decade-long fragmentation of Mexico's criminal landscape from 6 major cartels to over 200 criminal groups.

sources.name: InSight Crime
BBC

events.title: H1N1 Swine Flu Pandemic — Mexico's Veracruz as Origin Point
events.detail: The H1N1 influenza pandemic of 2009–2010 was traced to La Gloria, Veracruz, Mexico, where a novel influenza strain first caused illness in February 2009. Mexico City shut down completely — closing schools, churches, and restaurants — in April 2009 as the world's first major pandemic response of the 21st century. Mexico's rapid and transparent reporting to the WHO was internationally praised. The pandemic killed an estimated 12,000–17,000 Mexicans and set precedents that would influence COVID-19 responses.

sources.name: WHO — H1N1 Situation Report (2009)
SSA (Secretaría de Salud) — Informe H1N1 2009

events.title: Zetas Formally Split from Gulf Cartel — Northern Mexico Descends into Chaos
events.detail: The Zetas paramilitary organization — originally the Gulf Cartel's enforcement arm, recruited from elite Mexican Army special forces in the late 1990s — formally severed ties with their Gulf Cartel patrons in February 2010. The split triggered immediate and catastrophic violence across Tamaulipas, Nuevo León, Coahuila, and Veracruz. The Zetas, led by Heriberto Lazcano ('El Lazca'), pioneered extreme public violence including beheadings, mass kidnappings, and the 72-migrant San Fernando massacre (August 2010). The Zetas' operational model of territorial control through terror was subsequently adopted by other groups.

sources.name: InSight Crime — Zetas profile (2012)
CIDE — Diagnóstico sobre la seguridad pública en México (2010)

events.title: Oaxaca APPO Commune — Six-Month Teacher Strike Turns Into Proto-Revolution
events.detail: In June 2006, Oaxaca teachers (Section 22 of SNTE) launched an annual strike for wage increases. After Governor Ruiz's police violently attempted to evict them (June 14), hundreds of civil society organizations joined to form the Popular Assembly of Oaxaca Peoples (APPO), which effectively controlled much of Oaxaca city for six months. The APPO occupation featured neighborhood radio stations, community governance, and feminist brigades. Federal police (PFP) retook the city in October 2006. The episode prefigured AMLO's social base and the political conditions for the 2018 election.

sources.name: Diana Denham & C.A.S.A. Collective, Teaching Rebellion (2008)
CNDH — Informe sobre conflicto en Oaxaca (2006)

events.title: Peña Nieto Inaugurated — PRI Returns to Power After 12-Year PAN Interval
events.detail: Enrique Peña Nieto (PRI) was inaugurated on December 1, 2012, ending 12 years of PAN presidencies. The PRI's return came with a reformist agenda — the Pacto por México — that secured cooperation from PAN and PRD for major structural reforms. Peña Nieto's first year was rated one of the most productive in legislative terms since the Cárdenas era. However, by 2014 his presidency collapsed under the Ayotzinapa crisis, corruption scandals, and economic stagnation.

sources.name: Los Pinos — Discurso de toma de posesión, December 1, 2012
Reforma — Cobertura especial Peña Nieto (2012)

events.title: Energy Reform Ends 75-Year Pemex Monopoly — Constitutional Amendment
events.detail: Peña Nieto's energy reform (constitutional amendments to Articles 25, 27, 28) ended PEMEX's 75-year oil monopoly, opening exploration and production to private and foreign investment for the first time since 1938. The reform was presented as the cornerstone of Mexico's modernization. First private oil-field auctions in 2015 attracted BP, Shell, and others. AMLO campaigned against the reform, calling it a 'betrayal of Cárdenas's legacy,' and his 2021-2024 re-nationalization partially reversed it.

sources.name: Diario Oficial de la Federación — Reforma Energética, December 20, 2013
George Baker, Mexico Energy Report (2014)

events.title: Ayotzinapa: 43 Normalista Students Disappear in Iguala, Guerrero
events.detail: On the night of September 26–27, 2014, students from the Ayotzinapa Rural Teachers' College were attacked by Iguala police — on orders of mayor José Luis Abarca — who handed 43 students to the Guerreros Unidos cartel. Three students were killed at the scene; 43 disappeared. The Peña Nieto government's 'historical truth' (mass incineration at Cocula) was scientifically refuted. Mass protests swept Mexico; #YaMeCansé and #FueElEstado trended globally. The case remains Mexico's most prominent human rights crisis.

sources.name: GIEI (Grupo Interdisciplinario de Expertos Independientes) — Informe 1 (2015)
OHCHR — Mexico Ayotzinapa Special Review

events.title: El Chapo Escapes Maximum Security Prison Through 1.5 km Tunnel
events.detail: Joaquín 'El Chapo' Guzmán escaped from Altiplano maximum security prison on July 11, 2015, through a 1.5 km tunnel equipped with ventilation, lighting, and a motorcycle on rails. The escape — his second prison break — was a catastrophic embarrassment for Peña Nieto. The tunnel had been dug under the nose of prison authorities, fueling allegations of systematic bribery at the highest level. El Chapo was recaptured in Los Mochis, Sinaloa, on January 8, 2016 and extradited to the US in 2017.

sources.name: SEGOB / PGR — Comunicado sobre fuga del Altiplano (July 2015)
Reuters — El Chapo tunnel escape (July 12, 2015)

events.title: El Chapo Recaptured — Sinaloa Cartel Succession Begins
events.detail: Joaquín 'El Chapo' Guzmán is recaptured by Mexican marines in Los Mochis, Sinaloa, six months after his dramatic tunnel escape from Altiplano prison. His extradition to the US in 2017 effectively transfers leadership to El Mayo Zambada and the Chapitos (sons), setting the stage for the 2024 internal conflict.

sources.name: AP
Reuters

events.title: NAFTA Renegotiation — Trump Threatens Withdrawal; Becomes USMCA
events.detail: President Trump launched renegotiation of NAFTA in May 2017, threatening withdrawal and tariffs. Mexico-US-Canada negotiations ran through 2017–2018, with the new USMCA signed September 30, 2018 (entering force July 1, 2020). Key changes: increased North American content requirements for autos (75%), labor provisions requiring Mexico to allow genuine union elections, Chapter 22 for state-owned enterprise competition, sunset clause every 16 years. The renegotiation solidified Mexico's position as the most exposed economy to US trade policy shifts.

sources.name: SRE — USMCA Negotiating Record
USTR — USMCA Summary (2020)

events.title: Telecom Reform Breaks Slim Monopoly — Creates Competition in Mexico
events.detail: The Peña Nieto telecom reform, passed June 2013, created a new regulatory body (IFT), declared Telmex and TV Azteca-Televisa as dominant operators with asymmetric regulations, broke the Slim fixed-line monopoly, and opened the market to virtual operators. OECD had estimated the Slim telecommunications monopoly cost Mexico $25 billion/year in lost economic efficiency. The reform was hailed by the OECD and World Bank as one of Latin America's most significant regulatory reforms, enabling mobile competition that cut data prices by 60% within three years.

sources.name: IFT — Resolución de preponderancia Telmex/Telcel (2014)
OECD — Telecommunication Policy Review Mexico (2012)

events.title: September 19, 2017 Earthquake (7.1 M) — Strikes on Anniversary of 1985 Disaster
events.detail: A 7.1 magnitude earthquake struck just 120 km south of Mexico City on September 19, 2017 — the 32nd anniversary of the 1985 earthquake, on the day of the annual earthquake drill. At least 370 people were killed; dozens of buildings collapsed including several schools. Toño the dog became a media hero searching rubble. The earthquake reinforced the lessons of 1985: citizen-organized rescue brigades (brigadas) mobilized faster than official services. President Peña Nieto's government response was judged more effective than in 1985 but still criticized for slow coordination.

sources.name: CENAPRED — Sismo del 19 de septiembre de 2017
Reuters — Mexico earthquake coverage (September 2017)

events.title: Gasolinazo — 20% Fuel Price Hike Triggers Nationwide Protests and Looting
events.detail: On January 1, 2017, the Peña Nieto government lifted gasoline price subsidies as part of the energy reform liberalization, causing prices to jump 14–20% overnight. The 'Gasolinazo' triggered the most widespread civil unrest in Mexico since the 1980s: roadblocks across 25 states, looting of 1,600+ stores, 400+ arrests, and at least 3 deaths. The crisis severely damaged Peña Nieto's popularity (below 15% approval) and mobilized the political base that gave AMLO his 2018 landslide victory.

sources.name: Reforma — Encuesta de aprobación presidencial (January 2017)
SENER — Liberalización de precios de gasolina (2017)

events.title: CJNG Debuts as 'Matazetas' — Jalisco New Generation Cartel Emerges
events.detail: 35 alleged Zeta cartel members are found dumped on the Boca del Río highway in Veracruz with a narco-banner from the 'Matazetas.' This is CJNG's public debut, framing itself as a vigilante force against the Zetas. El Mencho's cartel would grow into the most powerful in Mexico within a decade.

sources.name: El Universal
InSight Crime

events.title: El Culiacanazo — Mexico Releases Ovidio Guzmán Under Cartel Pressure
events.detail: Mexican security forces attempt to arrest Ovidio Guzmán, son of El Chapo and key Sinaloa leader, in Culiacán. The Sinaloa Cartel responds with roadblocks, vehicle fires, and armed attacks across the city, forcing the government to release Ovidio to prevent a massacre. The episode is a profound embarrassment for President AMLO and reveals the state's limits against cartel military capacity.

sources.name: BBC
Reuters

events.title: Ovidio Guzmán Arrested in Culiacán — Four Years After El Culiacanazo
events.detail: Four years after the October 2019 Culiacanazo (when Mexico released Ovidio under cartel pressure), AMLO ordered a second operation on January 5, 2023. This time the government held firm despite intense cartel violence — 29 deaths, burned vehicles, airport shutdown, 29 soldiers captured then released. Ovidio was successfully transferred to a maximum security prison and later extradited to the US in July 2023 to face fentanyl trafficking charges. The operation demonstrated AMLO's hardened posture in his final year but cost him politically.

sources.name: SEDENA / Reuters — January 5, 2023
US DOJ — Ovidio Guzmán López extradition (July 2023)

events.title: AMLO Takes Office — 'Hugs Not Bullets' Security Strategy
events.detail: Andrés Manuel López Obrador (AMLO) is inaugurated as president with a radical shift in security policy: 'abrazos, no balazos' (hugs, not bullets). He dissolves the Federal Police, creates the National Guard, and deprioritizes armed confrontation with cartels in favor of social programs. Critics argue the policy empowers cartels by reducing pressure.

sources.name: Reuters
BBC

events.title: AMLO's Judicial Reform Passes — Judges to Be Elected by Popular Vote
events.detail: In his final weeks in office, AMLO secured passage of a major constitutional reform requiring all federal judges, including Supreme Court justices, to be elected by popular vote. The reform, passed with Morena's supermajority, was fiercely opposed by bar associations, the US government (Ambassador Salazar called it a threat to bilateral relations), and the Supreme Court itself. Multiple justices resigned in protest. The reform fundamentally alters Mexico's separation of powers and has triggered USMCA investor concerns.

sources.name: Diario Oficial de la Federación — Reforma Judicial, September 15, 2024
AP — Mexico judicial reform (September 2024)

events.title: El Mayo Zambada Arrested in Texas — Sinaloa Cartel Civil War Erupts
events.detail: Ismael 'El Mayo' Zambada, co-founder and leader of the Sinaloa Cartel, is arrested in El Paso, Texas after being lured onto a plane by Joaquín Guzmán López (son of El Chapo, 'El Chapito'). El Mayo claims he was kidnapped; the Chapitos faction denies it. The incident fractures the Sinaloa Cartel into two warring factions — Los Chapitos vs. Los Mayos — triggering catastrophic violence in Culiacán that kills hundreds and displaces tens of thousands.

sources.name: US DOJ
Reuters
AP

events.title: Tren Maya Inaugurated — 1,525 km Railway Across Maya Heartland
events.detail: President AMLO inaugurated the Tren Maya (Maya Train) passenger rail on December 16, 2023. The 1,525-km loop railway crosses five states (Campeche, Chiapas, Quintana Roo, Tabasco, Yucatán) connecting the Yucatán Peninsula's tourist and archaeological sites.
